ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Beijiang or Xinjiang-style lamb skewer; closest parent 'lamb kebab food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for spiced lamb skewer; A0FKP (Lamb meat, grilled) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Beijiang lamb skewer; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Beijiang lamb skewer typically weighs approximately 30–40 g of cooked lamb meat on a single bamboo or metal skewer, as served at Northern Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, composite of retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 in fat, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked broiled lamb (composite retail cuts, lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 in fat). Beijiang lamb skewers are grilled over charcoal with cumin, chilli, and sesame seasoning; the seasoning contributes minimal macronutrient change per 100 g but increases sodium. Sodium value is adjusted upward to reflect typical seasoning levels consistent with USDA data for seasoned grilled lamb. Values represent the cooked, as-served state.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pure Protein and Fat Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beijiang lamb skewer is a protein- and fat-dominant food with negligible carbohydrate content (0 g per 100 g). Pure meat and fat foods have a glycemic index of effectively zero as they contain no glycemic carbohydrates. Per University of Sydney GI Database convention and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), foods with no digestible carbohydrate are assigned GI = 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and GL = 0. glycemic_index is set to 1 and gi_category to 'low' as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates. glycemic_load_per_serving = 0.0 confirmed.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning (cumin, salt, chilli)
hypertension.guidance: Each skewer contributes approximately 133 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional dipping sauces to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content; high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beijiang lamb skewer contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of effectively zero (set to 1 per convention), making it a low-glycemic-impact protein source suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol content from lamb
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 35 g skewer provides approximately 2.4 g saturated fat and 34 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat intake and choose leaner cuts or smaller portions in line with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijiang Lamb Skewer (羊肉串, Yángròu chuàn) is a classic Northern Chinese street food originating from the Xinjiang and Beijiang regions of China, widely popularised across mainland China and now available in Singapore at Northern Chinese specialty restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant. The dish consists of small cubes or strips of lamb — typically from the shoulder, leg, or rib — threaded onto bamboo or metal skewers and grilled over high-heat charcoal. The lamb is seasoned generously with ground cumin, dried chilli flakes, coarse salt, and sesame seeds, which are the defining spice profile of Xinjiang-style grilled meat. The exterior develops a lightly charred, smoky crust while the interior remains tender and juicy. The flavour is bold, savoury, and aromatic with a distinctive cumin-forward warmth and mild heat from the chilli. Nutritionally, the skewer is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content, making it a low-glycemic-impact option. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 258 kcal, 25.6 g protein, and 16.8 g fat. The sodium content is moderate due to the seasoning blend. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and when prepared with halal-certified lamb, it is suitable for halal diets. Clinically, it is a good protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its effectively zero glycemic load (GI set to 1), but saturated fat and sodium levels warrant portion moderation for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension.
common_user_queries: What spices are used in Xinjiang lamb skewer?
Is lamb skewer suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked broiled lamb (composite retail cuts), which is the closest approved-source match for this preparation. No exact entry for Beijiang or Xinjiang-style lamb skewer exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. The adaptation is defensible given the similar preparation method (high-heat grilling of lamb with dry seasoning), but confidence is medium rather than high because the exact spice blend and fat trim level of the restaurant product are not independently verified.
notes: carbohydrate_g is 0.0 because lamb is a pure protein-and-fat food containing no digestible carbohydrate; this is naturally zero and confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. sugar_g is 0.0 for the same reason — naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a pure meat product with no plant-based fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero in cooked meat. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for this cut. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) and gi_category to 'low' as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; glycemic_load_per_serving = 0.0 confirmed. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all populated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked broiled lamb. Sodium is adjusted upward from the USDA baseline to reflect typical seasoning (cumin, salt, chilli) used in Beijiang-style preparation. The cuisine_tag 'Noodle' in the input is incorrect for this dish and has been disregarded; this is a grilled meat product. GI source note: per University of Sydney GI Database convention, pure meat foods with zero carbohydrate are assigned GI = 1; gi_category is 'low' by convention.
